importedJiraIssues.summary: Epic: Discovery, search and personalization foundation
importedJiraIssues.description:
Goal: strengthen Hobbeast's core discovery experience without regressing current working filters.

Scope:

* preserve current working event filtering UX
* improve personal recommendation logic
* improve category persistence and filter state handling
* improve relevance ranking for events
* support external event source blending in discovery

Success criteria:

* category, personal and free-text filters can switch without losing user selections
* events are ranked more meaningfully for the user
* external sources can appear in the same discovery surface without breaking the current Hobbeast experience

Implementation backfill note:

The Android implementation track already moved discovery significantly forward with explicit discovery lenses, category/search/distance/venue filtering, richer event-card journey semantics, imported-vs-first-party posture and community-aware empty/sparse-data handling. This epic should therefore be treated as partially concretized by implementation, not only conceptual.

Cross-surface follow-up:

Web must now align with the same discovery/filter/card semantics to avoid drift. Follow-up story: HOB-201.

importedJiraIssues.summary: Epic: Organizer tools and attendee operations
importedJiraIssues.description:
Goal: extend Hobbeast with organizer-focused capabilities inspired by InviteM, without regressing the current community-first experience.

Scope:

* explicit Organizer mode / organizer-facing surfaces and permissions
* attendee management
* capacity and waitlist
* event communication and reminders
* check-in ready event lifecycle
* organizer-specific dashboard surfaces

Success criteria:

* organizers can manage attendees and event participation more effectively
* an explicit organizer-facing operating mode can be introduced without harming the current Hobbeast simplicity
* event operations are possible without losing Hobbeast's current simplicity

Implementation backfill note:

The Android implementation track already pushed forward attendee roster handling, status filters, waitlist posture, check-in runtime, invite-code handling, audit-aware operations, first organizer run guidance and organizer operational readouts. This epic is therefore partially concretized by implementation.

Cross-surface follow-up:

Web must align to the same participant-state, check-in and organizer runtime semantics. Follow-up story: HOB-202.

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: A fő eseményszűrők állapotkezelése legyen konzisztens és perzisztens
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: Discovery, search and personalization foundation
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Ensure the main event filters behave as mutually exclusive primary modes while preserving category selections across mode switches.

h1. Problem statement

The current Events page mixes two concerns:

# Which *primary discovery mode* is active ({{search}}, {{all}}, {{personal}}, {{categories}})
# Which *category selections* are stored in memory

This causes regressions where category chips remain selected in memory but are not visually or functionally re-activated when the user returns to the Categories mode.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Primary filter model

* Only one primary filter can be active at a time:
** {{Search}}
** {{All}}
** {{For me}}
** {{Categories}}
* Switching primary filter must *not* clear stored category selections.
* Category selections are only cleared by:
** {{Clear selections}}
** new session / full reset logic

h2. Category persistence

* Persist selected items in dedicated state buckets:
** {{selectedCategoryIds}}
** {{selectedSubcategoryKeys}}
** {{selectedActivityKeys}}
* Persist expanded tree state separately from selected state.
* When {{Categories}} is re-activated, previously selected items must:
** render as active (green)
** apply filtering immediately
** reopen relevant parent branches if needed

h2. Search mode

* Typing in the free text input activates {{Search}} mode.
* Clearing the input returns the page to {{All}} unless the user explicitly reselects another primary mode.
* Search mode must not delete stored category selections.

h2. Personal mode

* {{For me}} mode must use favorite categories / hobbies / joined history logic.
* Entering {{For me}} mode must not delete stored category selections.

h1. Technical design

h2. Frontend state contract

{noformat} type PrimaryFilter = 'all' | 'search' | 'personal' | 'categories'

 const [primaryFilter, setPrimaryFilter] = useState<PrimaryFilter>('all')
 const [search, setSearch] = useState('')
 const [selectedCategoryIds, setSelectedCategoryIds] = useState<Set<string>>(new Set())
 const [selectedSubcategoryKeys, setSelectedSubcategoryKeys] = useState<Set<string>>(new Set())
 const [selectedActivityKeys, setSelectedActivityKeys] = useState<Set<string>>(new Set())
 const [expandedCategoryIds, setExpandedCategoryIds] = useState<Set<string>>(new Set()){noformat}

h2. Derived behaviour

* {{primaryFilter}} decides which filtering pipeline is executed.
* selected category states decide chip active styling and category filter payload.
* category filtering only runs when {{primaryFilter === 'categories'}}.

h2. Filtering pipeline order

# base event source list
# source filter ({{All sources / Hobbeast / External}})
# distance filter
# primary filter branch:
#* {{all}} -> no extra restriction
#* {{search}} -> text match
#* {{personal}} -> personal recommendation rule set
#* {{categories}} -> category tree matching
# sort / ranking

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Keress eseményt [________________________]                   |
|                                                              |
| [Mind] [Nekem] [Kategóriák]                                  |
|                                                              |
| Active primary mode: KATEGÓRIÁK                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+

Kategóriák modal
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Kategóriák                                  [Törlés] [Kész]  |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| > Gasztronómia                                               |
|   [x] Étterem                                                |
|   [x] Kávézó                                                 |
|     [x] Specialty                                            |
|     [ ] Brunch                                               |
| > Sport                                                      |
|   [ ] Futás                                                  |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* exactly one primary mode is active at any time
* category selections survive switching to {{All}}, {{For me}}, or {{Search}}
* returning to {{Categories}} restores visual active state and actual filtering
* selected items are green/active without requiring reselection
* {{Clear selections}} resets both stored selections and active category filtering
* no regression in source filter or distance filter behaviour

h1. QA scenarios

# Select 3 categories -> switch to {{For me}} -> switch back to {{Categories}} -> selections still active and filtering applied
# Select categories -> type text search -> clear text -> categories remain stored but inactive until {{Categories}} is clicked again
# Select categories -> click {{Clear selections}} -> all category states cleared
# Use distance filter + categories together -> result set remains stable and predictable

importedJiraIssues.summary: Epic: RSVP, ticketing and check-in lifecycle
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Introduce a richer RSVP, ticketing-light and check-in-ready lifecycle for Hobbeast events.

h1. Functional scope

* support participation states beyond join/unsubscribe
* support capacity-aware admission
* support lightweight ticket and admission control extensions
* provide a future-safe contract for QR / code-based check-in

h1. Participation state model

Supported states:

* {{interested}}
* {{going}}
* {{waitlist}}
* {{checked_in}}
* {{cancelled}}
* {{no_show}}

Rules:

* {{going}} requires available capacity, unless organizer override exists
* if capacity is full and waitlist enabled, new attendee enters {{waitlist}}
* {{checked_in}} only valid from {{going}}
* {{no_show}} only valid after event start/end workflow
* organizer can manually transition {{waitlist -> going}}

h1. Ticketing-light scope

* support {{participation_type = external_ticket}}
* support {{external_ticket_url}}
* support {{ticket_token}} / {{invite_code}} placeholders for internal admission control
* support future {{ticket_tier_id}} reference without implementing full payment now

h1. Check-in scope

* check-in method can be:
** manual organizer check-in
** QR token scan
** invite code entry
* check-in surface must validate:
** event id
** attendee identity / token
** current participation status
** duplicate check-in prevention

h1. Suggested backend entities

{noformat}EventParticipation {
  id: uuid
  event_id: uuid
  user_id: uuid
  status: 'interested' | 'going' | 'waitlist' | 'checked_in' | 'cancelled' | 'no_show'
  joined_at: timestamptz
  checked_in_at: timestamptz | null
  invite_code: string | null
  ticket_token: string | null
}{noformat}

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}Event detail card
+---------------------------------------------------+
| Friday Board Game Night                           |
| 18 / 24 spots filled                              |
| Status: GOING                                     |
|                                                   |
| [Interested] [Going] [Leave]                      |
| Waitlist enabled                                  |
+---------------------------------------------------+

Organizer check-in view
+---------------------------------------------------+
| Check-in: Friday Board Game Night                 |
+---------------------------------------------------+
| Search attendee [________________________]        |
| Or scan QR                                        |
|                                                   |
| John Doe          GOING        [Check in]         |
| Anna Smith        WAITLIST     [Promote]          |
+---------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* participation lifecycle supports interested / going / waitlist / checked_in / cancelled / no_show
* waitlist promotion logic is possible
* duplicate check-in is prevented
* external ticket URL can be associated to an event
* future ticket tiers can plug into this lifecycle without redesign

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add trending, featured and early-access discovery surfaces
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: Discovery, search and personalization foundation
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Define the discovery surfaces and ranking logic for {{Trending}}, {{Featured}}, and {{Early access}} event sections on the Events page.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Discovery sections

The Events page can expose separate horizontally or vertically stacked sections:

* Trending nearby
* Featured events
* Early access / limited spots
* Because you joined / Because you like

h2. Ranking contracts

h3. Trending nearby

Inputs:

* recent views
* recent detail opens
* recent join clicks
* recent confirmed {{going}}
* distance from user/profile location
* time decay (recent interactions weigh more)

Suggested formula example:

{noformat}trending_score =
  (views * 0.1) +
  (detail_opens * 0.3) +
  (join_clicks * 0.6) +
  (going_count * 1.0) -
  distance_penalty -
  stale_event_penalty{noformat}

h3. Featured events

* manually curated by admin/organizer flag
* optional future paid promotion flag
* featured ordering by explicit {{featured_priority}}

h3. Early access / limited spots

* events with {{visibility_type = early_access_limited}}
* or events with small remaining capacity threshold
* can show {{N spots left}} badge

h2. UI rules

* these sections must not break the existing flat list and primary filters
* section cards reuse the main event card contract where possible
* if not enough events exist for a section, section can be hidden

h1. Suggested data model extensions

{noformat}Event {
  is_featured: boolean
  featured_priority: number | null
  visibility_type: 'public' | 'private' | 'invite_only' | 'early_access_limited'
}{noformat}

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Trending nearby                                               |
| [Card] [Card] [Card]                                          |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Featured                                                      |
| [Featured card] [Featured card]                               |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Early access / limited spots                                  |
| [Only 5 spots left] [Invite-only preview]                     |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* discovery sections have explicit inclusion and ranking rules
* trending score is explainable and adjustable
* featured section supports manual curation
* early access section supports limited-availability visibility logic

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Improve external event rendering and blending in the main events list
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: External event integrations and marketplace expansion
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Render external events inside the main Hobbeast event list without breaking native event UX or source filtering.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Supported source types

* hobbeast native events
* eventbrite events
* ticketmaster events
* seatgeek events
* future external providers using {{external_events}}

h2. Rendering contract

Required card fields:

* source label / badge
* title
* short description
* city / address summary
* start date / time
* category summary
* price summary if available
* CTA button:
** native event -> open internal detail page
** external event -> open external_url or mapped detail proxy

h2. Behaviour rules

* external events must appear in mixed lists when source filter allows them
* external events must appear under {{External}} source filter even if provider differs
* source-specific visual badge required ({{Ticketmaster}}, {{Eventbrite}}, {{SeatGeek}})
* own event controls must never appear on external event cards
* join / unsubscribe CTA must not appear for unmanaged external providers unless explicitly supported later

h2. Mapping contract

{noformat}DisplayEvent {
  id: string
  source: 'hobbeast' | 'eventbrite' | 'ticketmaster' | 'seatgeek' | 'external'
  sourceBadge: string
  title: string
  description: string | null
  city: string | null
  address: string | null
  startsAt: string | null
  category: string | null
  imageUrl: string | null
  externalUrl: string | null
  isExternal: boolean
}{noformat}

h2. Fallback rules

* if category missing -> render {{Other}}
* if image missing -> render generic placeholder
* if external_url missing -> disable CTA and mark record as incomplete
* if date missing -> event hidden from public list unless fallback policy exists

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| [Ticketmaster] Friday Jazz Night                             |
| Budapest • 2026-04-12 19:00                                  |
| Live music, downtown venue                                   |
| From 4500 HUF                                                |
|                                              [Open source]   |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* external cards render consistently beside native cards
* source filter works for all external providers
* source badge and CTA behaviour are deterministic per provider
* incomplete external records follow fallback rules instead of silently breaking UI

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Integrate Ticketmaster sync and normalization into the external event ingestion pipeline
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: External event integrations and marketplace expansion
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Implement a production-ready Ticketmaster ingestion flow that normalizes provider data into Hobbeast {{external_events}} records.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Request modes

* {{search_preview}}
* {{sync}}

h2. Provider fetch contract

* use Ticketmaster Discovery API
* support filters:
** countryCode
** city
** classificationName
** keyword
** startDateTime / safe date filtering
** page
** size

h2. Mapping rules

{noformat}external_source = 'ticketmaster'
external_id = tm.id
external_url = tm.url
title = tm.name
description = tm.info || tm.pleaseNote
category = classifications.segment.name
subcategory = classifications.genre.name || classifications.subGenre.name
location_city = venue.city.name
location_address = venue.address.line1 || venue.name
location_lat = venue.location.latitude
location_lon = venue.location.longitude
event_date = dates.start.localDate
event_time = dates.start.localTime
price_min = priceRanges[0].min
price_max = priceRanges[0].max
currency = priceRanges[0].currency
organizer_name = promoter.name
image_url = images[0].url{noformat}

h2. Deduplication rules

* unique key: {{(external_source, external_id)}}
* upsert on sync
* retain latest {{source_payload}} and {{source_last_synced_at}}

h2. Failure handling

* malformed records must be skipped with structured log entry
* HTTP/provider errors must return diagnosable payload
* zero-result response must not be treated as technical failure

h2. Operational notes

* function must support preview mode without DB writes
* sync mode must return inserted/updated count
* provider secrets must be read from environment / Supabase secrets

h1. Sequence

{noformat}Client/Test trigger
 -> Edge Function
 -> Ticketmaster API fetch
 -> map/normalize
 -> dedupe/upsert into external_events
 -> response summary{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* preview mode returns normalized event payloads without DB writes
* sync mode upserts normalized records into {{external_events}}
* duplicate provider records are not duplicated in DB
* zero-result case is distinguishable from provider failure

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Integrate SeatGeek as a second external event source
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: External event integrations and marketplace expansion
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Implement a production-ready SeatGeek ingestion flow normalized into Hobbeast {{external_events}}.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Request modes

* {{search_preview}}
* {{sync}}

h2. Provider fetch contract

* support SeatGeek client credentials / public API contract as available
* support query parameters:
** q
** page
** per_page
** optional geographic filters when supported

h2. Mapping rules

{noformat}external_source = 'seatgeek'
external_id = sg.id
title = sg.title
external_url = sg.url
description = sg.description || null
category = sg.type || sg.taxonomies[0]?.name || null
location_city = sg.venue.city
location_address = sg.venue.address || sg.venue.name
location_lat = sg.venue.location.lat
location_lon = sg.venue.location.lon
event_date = derived from sg.datetime_utc / local date mapping
event_time = derived from sg.datetime_utc / local time mapping
price_min = sg.stats.lowest_price || null
price_max = sg.stats.highest_price || null

image_url = sg.performers[0]?.image || null{noformat}

h2. Deduplication rules

* unique key: {{(external_source, external_id)}}

h2. Failure handling

* malformed record skip with structured log
* zero results distinct from provider failure

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add attendee management tools for event organizers
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: Organizer tools and attendee operations
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Provide a concrete organizer-facing attendee management view for a single event with filterable participant states and actionable row-level controls.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Attendee list surface

* organizer can open attendee management from owned event detail page or Organizer mode
* attendee list columns:
** display_name
** email (if available and permission-safe)
** participation_status
** joined_at
** checked_in_at
** ticket tier / access type if present
* list must support pagination and server-side filtering for large events

h2. Filters

* status filters:
** all
** interested
** going
** waitlist
** checked_in
** cancelled
** no_show
* free-text search by display_name / email
* optional sort by joined_at / status / checked_in_at

h2. Row actions

* promote waitlist -> going
* move going -> cancelled
* undo check-in
* open attendee detail drawer
* add organizer note on attendee record

h2. Attendee detail drawer

Required blocks:

* basic identity summary
* participation timeline
* check-in history
* organizer notes
* future messaging shortcuts

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add capacity and waitlist handling to event participation
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: RSVP, ticketing and check-in lifecycle
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Define capacity enforcement and waitlist promotion behaviour for Hobbeast event participation.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Capacity model

* event can define {{max_capacity}}
* event can define {{waitlist_enabled}}
* effective free slots = {{max_capacity - going_count}}
* {{interested}} users do not consume capacity
* {{going}} users consume capacity
* {{checked_in}} remains counted under admitted attendees

h2. Entry rules

* if free slot available and user confirms participation -> status {{going}}
* if no free slot and waitlist enabled -> status {{waitlist}}
* if no free slot and waitlist disabled -> show full-state error

h2. Promotion rules

* organizer can manually promote waitlist attendee to {{going}}
* optional future auto-promotion hook can be supported when slot frees up
* promotion must fail if no free slot exists and no override is allowed

h2. Visibility rules

* event detail page must show:
** current admitted count
** capacity limit
** waitlist enabled state
* organizer panel must show:
** going count
** waitlist count
** remaining slots

h1. Suggested data model fields

{noformat}Event {
  max_capacity: number | null
  waitlist_enabled: boolean
}{noformat}

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}+---------------------------------------------------+
| Friday Board Game Night                           |
| 18 / 24 spots filled                              |
| Waitlist: enabled                                 |
|                                                   |
| [Join]                                            |
+---------------------------------------------------+

Full state example
+---------------------------------------------------+
| 24 / 24 spots filled                              |
| Waitlist available                                |
| [Join waitlist]                                   |
+---------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* capacity logic differentiates between interested and going users
* waitlist entry works only when configured
* organizer can see remaining slots and waitlist counts
* UI communicates full/waitlist state clearly

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add richer RSVP states and participation lifecycle
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: RSVP, ticketing and check-in lifecycle
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Define the full participation lifecycle state machine for Hobbeast event attendance beyond the current join/unsubscribe model.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Supported states

* {{interested}}
* {{going}}
* {{waitlist}}
* {{checked_in}}
* {{cancelled}}
* {{no_show}}

h2. Allowed transitions

* default discovery action -> {{interested}}
* {{interested -> going}}
* {{interested -> cancelled}}
* {{going -> cancelled}}
* {{waitlist -> going}}
* {{going -> checked_in}}
* {{going -> no_show}} (after event time window)
* {{checked_in -> cancelled}} not allowed in standard path

h2. State semantics

* {{interested}}: lightweight interest, no capacity reserved
* {{going}}: confirmed slot, capacity reserved
* {{waitlist}}: pending slot, no capacity reserved
* {{checked_in}}: physically or logically admitted at event
* {{cancelled}}: user/organizer removed participation
* {{no_show}}: did not check in despite confirmed status

h2. UI rules

* event CTA area must render state-aware actions
* status badge must be visible on event detail page and organizer attendee screen
* own events must not show participation CTA as if user were an attendee-only user

h1. Suggested state machine contract

{noformat}type ParticipationStatus =
  | 'interested'
  | 'going'
  | 'waitlist'
  | 'checked_in'
  | 'cancelled'
  | 'no_show'{noformat}

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}Event detail status block
+---------------------------------------------------+
| Your status: INTERESTED                           |
|                                                   |
| [Mark as going] [Remove interest]                 |
+---------------------------------------------------+

Waitlist example
+---------------------------------------------------+
| Your status: WAITLIST                             |
| You will be notified if a slot becomes available. |
| [Leave waitlist]                                  |
+---------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* each participation state has explicit meaning
* allowed state transitions are documented and enforceable
* event UI can render state-specific controls and labels consistently

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Implement native event creation flow and validation rules

importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Define a developer-ready native event creation flow for Hobbeast with extensibility for paid, private and organizer-grade events.

h1. Functional requirements

h2. Form sections

# Basic info

* title (required, 3..120)
* short description (optional, max 280)
* long description (optional, markdown/plain text safe rendering)
* cover image URL/upload placeholder

# Classification

* category tree selection required
* support category, subcategory and activity-level selection
* store normalized category identifiers, not only display names

# Time and visibility

* event date required
* start time required
* end time optional
* timezone resolved from user profile or browser locale
* visibility type:
** public
** private
** invite_only
** early_access_limited

# Location

* city required
* address optional
* location autocomplete shared with profile flow
* location precision toggle:
** exact location visible to attendees
** only approximate location visible publicly

# Participation model

* participation type:
** free
** paid
** external_ticket
* max capacity optional
* waitlist enabled boolean
* organizer notes optional (internal, not public)
* entry window optional:
** entry_start_at
** entry_end_at

h1. Validation rules

* title required
* at least one normalized category path required
* city required
* if {{participation_type = paid}}, event must support pricing/ticket config entity
* if {{visibility = invite_only}}, invitation strategy must be selected later
* if {{max_capacity}} is set, must be integer > 0
* if both entry window fields present, {{entry_start_at < entry_end_at}}
* if end time present, {{start_at < end_at}}

h1. Data model notes

Suggested backend fields:

{noformat}Event {
  id: uuid
  title: string
  short_description: string | null
  description: string | null
  category_path: string[]
  visibility_type: 'public' | 'private' | 'invite_only' | 'early_access_limited'
  participation_type: 'free' | 'paid' | 'external_ticket'
  city: string
  address: string | null
  lat: number | null
  lon: number | null
  exact_location_visible: boolean
  starts_at: timestamptz
  ends_at: timestamptz | null
  max_capacity: number | null
  waitlist_enabled: boolean
  organizer_notes: string | null
  entry_start_at: timestamptz | null
  entry_end_at: timestamptz | null
}{noformat}

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Create event                                                 |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
| Title *                     [______________________________] |
| Short description           [______________________________] |
| Long description            [..............................] |
|                                                              |

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Introduce explicit Organizer mode navigation and permissions model
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Epic: Organizer tools and attendee operations
importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Create an explicit organizer-facing operating mode so users who own events can access organizer tools without overloading the default community discovery UX.

h1. Functional requirements

* show Organizer mode entry only for users with at least one owned event or organizer capability
* organizer mode entry points:
** profile menu switch
** event detail quick action for owned events
* organizer mode surfaces:
** My events
** Attendees
** Check-in
** Messages
** Analytics
* organizer mode must not replace the default discovery-first home for normal users

h1. Permissions model

* organizer-only actions require event ownership or organizer role assignment
* non-organizers must not see organizer controls for foreign events
* organizer mode visibility should be claim-based or ownership-derived

h1. Suggested frontend contract

{noformat}type AppMode = 'community' | 'organizer'

OrganizerCapability {
  can_manage_events: boolean
  can_check_in: boolean
  can_message_attendees: boolean
  can_view_event_analytics: boolean
}{noformat}

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}Top nav / profile menu
+--------------------------------+
| Henrik Faul                    |
| ------------------------------ |
| Community mode                 |
| Organizer mode   [switch]      |
| Profile                        |
| Sign out                       |
+--------------------------------+

Organizer dashboard shell
+--------------------------------------------------+
| Organizer mode                                   |
| [My events] [Attendees] [Check-in] [Messages]    |
| [Analytics]                                      |
+--------------------------------------------------+
| Event cards / stats / organizer tools            |
+--------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* organizer mode is only visible for eligible users
* organizer mode and community mode can coexist without route confusion
* organizer-only permissions are enforced in UI and backend

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add paid event pricing model and ticket tier configuration

importedJiraIssues.description:
h1. Objective

Support paid events with configurable ticket tiers, while remaining compatible with free and external-ticket event types.

h1. Functional requirements

* allow event organizer to define one or more ticket tiers
* tier fields:
** name
** description
** price_amount
** currency
** quantity_limit
** sale_start_at
** sale_end_at
** visibility (public / hidden / early_access_only)
* support free tier as explicit zero-price tier if needed
* support sorting tiers by display order

h1. Data model notes

{noformat}EventTicketTier {
  id: uuid
  event_id: uuid
  name: string
  description: string | null
  price_amount: number
  currency: string
  quantity_limit: number | null
  quantity_sold: number
  sale_start_at: timestamptz | null
  sale_end_at: timestamptz | null
  visibility: 'public' | 'hidden' | 'early_access_only'
  display_order: number
}{noformat}

h1. Validation rules

* {{price_amount >= 0}}
* {{quantity_limit}} nullable but if present must be > 0
* if both sale dates present, {{sale_start_at < sale_end_at}}
* hidden tiers cannot be visible in public event page by default

h1. UI wireframe

{noformat}Ticket tiers
+---------------------------------------------------+
| [Add tier]                                        |
+---------------------------------------------------+
| General admission                                 |
| Price: [4500 HUF]  Qty: [100]                     |
| Sale: [2026-04-01 10:00] -> [2026-04-10 18:00]    |
| Visibility: (o) Public ( ) Hidden ( ) Early only  |
|                                    [Save] [Delete]|
+---------------------------------------------------+{noformat}

h1. Acceptance criteria

* organizer can configure multiple ticket tiers per paid event
* hidden and early-access-only tiers are supported structurally
* ticket tiers can later be connected to payment flow without redesign

importedJiraIssues.description: Mapy.com trip planning core integration for Hobbeast. Build interactive map view, address and POI search, map click point selection, route planning with waypoints, route rendering, event-level route storage and attribution-safe provider usage.

importedJiraIssues.summary: Mapy.com trip planning AI and automation readiness
importedJiraIssues.description: Prepare the Mapy.com trip planning capability so it can later be used not only through direct user interaction but also through backend automation and AI agents. Define reusable service contracts, structured request and response schemas, deterministic location resolution and auditable automated route planning.

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Select and implement web map renderer for Mapy.com trip planning
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Create the map rendering foundation for the Hobbeast trip planner so Mapy.com tiles, route geometry and trip markers can be displayed in a fully interactive map view.

Problem:
The current planner has no real map view and requires manual coordinate entry. The Mapy REST API provides data and tiles, but a web map renderer must be selected and integrated.

Scope:

* select one supported web map library for production use
* initialize reusable TripPlanningMap component
* load Mapy raster map tiles
* support zoom, pan and fit bounds
* support overlays for markers and route polylines
* support responsive desktop and mobile rendering
* define map state handling for route edit mode

Technical requirements:

* do not use deprecated JS SDK approach
* use a reusable map wrapper component and route overlay layer structure
* support future layers for POI, elevation profile interaction and drag editing
* map container lifecycle must handle re-render and route updates correctly

UI requirements:

* embedded map view inside planner card/modal
* visible map controls for zoom
* route markers and route line must be visually distinct

Acceptance criteria:

* interactive map view renders inside trip planner
* Mapy map tiles are visible
* markers and polyline overlays can be drawn
* map resizes correctly on mobile
* selected renderer is documented in implementation notes

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Build address and POI search UX with Mapy suggest and geocoding
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Allow users to choose route points by typing addresses, place names and POIs instead of manually entering latitude and longitude.

Problem:
The current trip planner requires manual coordinate input for start and end points. This is not usable for normal users.

Scope:

* implement reusable location search input for start point, end point and waypoint entries
* integrate Mapy suggest-style search while typing
* resolve selected items into normalized location objects with coordinates
* support address, POI, city and generic place lookup cases
* handle loading, empty and error states
* support mobile-friendly result list interaction

Functional requirements:

* user types at least partial place name or address
* system shows suggestion list
* user selects one item
* selected item populates input and resolved coordinates are stored in planner state
* resolved points can immediately be used for routing

Data/state requirements:

* keep raw typed input separate from resolved location object
* normalized location object should include at minimum:
** display label
** provider identifier if available
** latitude
** longitude
** location type
** bounding box if available

Acceptance criteria:

* no manual coordinate entry is needed for standard point selection
* start and end point can be selected from search suggestions
* selected result stores resolved coordinates in planner state
* planner UX works on desktop and mobile

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Implement map click and reverse geocoding point selection
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Allow the user to click on the map and assign the clicked point as start, end or waypoint using reverse geocoding.

Problem:
Even with address search, a usable trip planner also needs direct map-based point selection for trails, viewpoints and places without obvious address entry.

Scope:

* listen to map click events
* place temporary marker on clicked position
* call reverse geocoding for the clicked point
* show a small selection action sheet or popup
* let the user assign the location as:
** start point
** end point
** waypoint
* support repeated edits and replacing existing points

Functional requirements:

* map click creates temporary candidate point
* system resolves a readable location label when possible
* user explicitly confirms target role for the point
* planner state updates immediately after confirmation

Technical requirements:

* reverse geocoding response must be normalized to the same location object format as search-selected points
* UI must handle cases where reverse geocode has only partial place data
* click-based selection must work on mobile touch interaction as well

Acceptance criteria:

* user can click the map to create route points
* clicked point can become start, end or waypoint
* the chosen point is stored in planner state with coordinates and readable label when available

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Implement route planning with start, end and waypoints
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Plan routes inside Hobbeast using Mapy routing with start point, end point and optional intermediate waypoints.

Problem:
The current prototype does not provide a complete route calculation flow from user-selected points.

Scope:

* build route request model from planner state
* support start, end and waypoint ordering
* support route type selection for hiking and other modes
* call provider routing endpoint
* parse route summary and geometry response
* support route recalculation when a point changes

Functional requirements:

* user selects route type
* user sets start and end points
* user optionally adds waypoints
* system calculates route and stores route result in planner state
* system handles no-route and invalid-point cases

Technical requirements:

* normalized route result should include at minimum:
** length
** duration
** geometry
** waypoint order
** provider metadata
* route logic must be reusable by future automation and AI callers

Acceptance criteria:

* route can be calculated from selected points
* waypoint order is respected
* route result contains summary and geometry data
* route can be recalculated after point edits

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Render route geometry, markers and route summary on interactive map
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Display the calculated route visually inside the trip planner with markers, polyline geometry and a summary panel.

Problem:
A route planning feature is not usable unless the user can clearly see the route path, point roles and basic route summary.

Scope:

* render start marker, end marker and waypoint markers
* render route polyline from routing response geometry
* fit map bounds to visible route
* show route summary panel with distance and duration
* support re-render on route recalculation
* support route clearing and route replacement

Functional requirements:

* start and end markers are visually distinct
* waypoint markers are clearly different from route endpoints
* route line updates after point edits or route type change
* summary panel reflects current route result

Technical requirements:

* geometry parsing must be isolated from the map rendering layer
* marker and polyline layers must be reusable in later route edit features
* route rendering must tolerate missing or partial provider response safely

Acceptance criteria:

* user sees the route path on the map
* start, end and waypoint roles are visually understandable
* summary panel shows current route distance and duration
* route refresh does not require page reload

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add elevation profile for hiking and biking routes
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Provide elevation-aware trip planning for hiking and biking routes so users can understand ascent, descent and terrain difficulty before saving a route.

Problem:
For hiking use cases, distance and duration alone are not enough. Route profile and elevation change are important planning criteria.

Scope:

* request elevation data for calculated route geometry
* sample route geometry where needed before elevation lookup
* display a basic elevation profile chart or structured summary
* show total ascent and total descent when derivable
* keep route usable if elevation enrichment fails

Functional requirements:

* elevation profile is available for hiking and biking route types
* planner shows route elevation summary after route calculation
* planner can fall back to route-only mode when elevation data is unavailable

Technical requirements:

* elevation enrichment must not block core route rendering
* geometry sampling strategy must be documented
* data model must support storing elevation profile or summary for saved trip plans

Acceptance criteria:

* a hiking or biking route can display elevation-related summary
* elevation lookup failure does not break core trip planning
* saved trip plan can retain elevation data when available

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Persist trip plan data in Hobbeast event model
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Allow a planned route to be saved, reloaded and edited as part of an event inside Hobbeast.

Problem:
A route planner is much less useful if the route cannot be stored together with the event that it belongs to.

Scope:

* define trip plan storage model
* link trip plan to event
* store route type, resolved points, waypoint list, route summary and geometry snapshot
* support edit existing trip plan flow
* support draft and saved route states
* prepare storage for optional elevation summary

Functional requirements:

* user can save a route as part of event creation or event editing
* previously saved route can be reopened for editing
* stored route survives page reload and later event modification

Technical requirements:

* trip plan must be stored in a normalized structure, not only UI state
* saved trip must preserve enough data for route preview without forcing fresh provider calls in every view
* provider metadata and snapshot versioning should be considered

Acceptance criteria:

* event can have a persisted trip plan record
* saved trip can be loaded and rendered again
* editing an existing trip plan updates the stored record correctly

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Implement Mapy attribution and provider-compliant trip planning UI
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Ensure that the Hobbeast trip planner uses Mapy.com data and map tiles in a provider-compliant way, with correct attribution visible in every relevant screen.

Problem:
Map integrations are not complete when provider attribution, copyright visibility and branding placement rules are ignored or inconsistently implemented.

Scope:

* define attribution rules for planner screens with map view
* define attribution rules for planner screens without map but with provider-powered location/routing UI
* display provider logo or powered-by marker in the correct visual context
* add required copyright text or footer block in the map area
* make attribution visible on desktop and mobile
* add QA checklist for compliance review

Functional requirements:

* attribution is visible when map tiles are rendered
* attribution remains visible when route summary overlays or floating controls are present
* planner UI can also show provider attribution when using provider-powered search and routing flows

Technical requirements:

* attribution component should be reusable and centrally configured
* the implementation should avoid scattered hardcoded logo placement
* responsive layout rules must prevent attribution from being hidden behind UI overlays

Acceptance criteria:

* every production planner screen that uses Mapy provider data shows provider attribution in the correct place
* mobile layout still displays the required attribution
* compliance rules are documented in implementation notes and QA checklist

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add Mapy route open and share actions for saved trip plans
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Let users open, share and export saved trip plans outside Hobbeast when that is useful for navigation, handoff or external route viewing.

Problem:
Users may want to continue navigation in Mapy, share the route with others, or open the route outside Hobbeast without rebuilding it.

Scope:

* generate provider-compatible open route links from saved trip data
* support opening the route in external Mapy web or app context where applicable
* support share-ready route URLs for user handoff
* support planner action buttons such as open externally and share
* support fallbacks when a full route URL cannot be generated from a degraded saved record

Functional requirements:

* user can open a saved trip outside Hobbeast
* user can copy/share a route link where supported
* route export action uses stored trip plan data and does not require re-entering points

Technical requirements:

* external route link generation should be encapsulated in one service/helper layer
* generated links must be deterministic from stored trip plan data
* unsupported or partial routes must fail gracefully with UI feedback

Acceptance criteria:

* saved trip plan can be opened externally through generated route link
* share action is available for supported trip records
* broken or incomplete route data is handled safely

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add robust error handling and observability for Mapy trip planning
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning core integration
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Make the trip planning capability diagnosable, resilient and user-friendly in the face of provider failures, invalid point combinations and partial route planning failures.

Problem:
Geocoding, routing and elevation integrations can fail independently. Without structured error handling the planner becomes unreliable and hard to support.

Scope:

* define error model for suggest, geocode, reverse geocode, routing and elevation steps
* provide user-safe UI messages for common failure cases
* log provider failures and partial failures in structured form
* distinguish no-result, invalid-route, provider-failure and degraded-mode cases
* support retry-ready error states where appropriate

Functional requirements:

* user sees understandable error feedback
* route planner can continue in degraded mode when non-critical enrichment fails
* technical diagnostics exist for debugging production issues

Technical requirements:

* use structured log payloads for provider integration failures
* preserve request context without exposing secrets in logs
* define common error object or service contract for planner flows

Acceptance criteria:

* provider failures are visible in logs and understandable in UI
* elevation or optional enrichment failure does not break core route planning
* no-result and hard-failure states are distinguishable

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Create backend trip planning service contract for non-UI callers
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning AI and automation readiness
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Create a backend service layer for Mapy-based trip planning that is independent from the React UI, so the same routing and location logic can be used by manual user flows, backend automation and future AI agents.

Business context:
If routing and location logic is embedded directly into UI components, the functionality becomes hard to reuse, test, automate and evolve. Hobbeast needs a backend-level trip planning contract that can be called by web UI, background jobs and agent workflows.

Problem statement:
The current trip planning implementation trend is UI-driven. This is sufficient for manual interaction, but not sufficient for future automation, AI-assisted route generation, scheduled route preparation or system-to-system orchestration.

Target architecture:
Create a provider-facing service module plus an application-facing orchestration service.

Expected layers:

# MapyProviderClient

* low-level HTTP integration
* handles auth, base URL, request shaping, retries, response parsing
* one method per provider capability

# TripPlanningService

* Hobbeast-facing application service
* normalizes request/response
* composes provider calls
* enforces validation and fallback rules
* reusable from UI endpoints, jobs and agent endpoints

# Transport layer / API endpoints

* UI endpoint(s)
* internal service endpoint(s)
* future agent endpoint(s)

Scope:

* define normalized service methods for:
** suggestLocation
** geocodeLocation
** reverseGeocodePoint
** planRoute
** enrichElevation
** buildExternalRouteLink
* define shared request and response schemas
* define validation rules and common error model
* define logging and correlation-id expectations
* define configuration handling and provider secrets usage
* define synchronous vs asynchronous boundaries
* define compatibility rules for UI and non-UI callers

Out of scope:

* final AI decision engine
* route recommendation ranking logic
* offline navigation
* full agent orchestration runtime

Detailed functional requirements:

# Service must accept normalized inputs instead of UI component state
# Service must not depend on browser-only APIs
# Service must return deterministic typed responses
# Service must use a common error format across suggest/geocode/routing/elevation
# Service must be callable from:
#* event create/update workflow
#* route planner UI actions
#* future automation jobs
#* future AI-agent workflows
# Service must support partial-success scenarios, for example:
#* route success + elevation failure
#* reverse geocode partial result
# Service must support idempotent read-like operations

Detailed technical requirements:

* define TypeScript interfaces in a shared domain module
* isolate Mapy-specific HTTP details from application logic
* use structured error types or tagged error objects
* attach request correlation id to logs
* sanitize provider responses before returning them to upper layers
* do not leak secrets or raw credentials in logs or returned payloads
* keep provider-specific raw response optional and internal by default

Suggested interface contract:

{noformat}export interface LocationRef {
  label: string
  lat: number
  lon: number
  type?: 'address' | 'poi' | 'city' | 'custom' | 'unknown'
  providerId?: string
  raw?: unknown
}

export interface TripPlanningRequest {
  routeType: 'foot_hiking' | 'foot_fast' | 'bike_road' | 'bike_mountain' | 'car_fast' | 'car_short'
  start: LocationRef
  end: LocationRef
  waypoints?: LocationRef[]
  options?: {
    avoidToll?: boolean
    avoidHighways?: boolean
    departureAt?: string
  }
}

export interface TripPlanningResult {
  routeType: string
  lengthM: number | null
  durationS: number | null
  geometry: unknown
  start: LocationRef
  end: LocationRef
  waypoints: LocationRef[]
  elevationSummary?: {
    ascentM?: number
    descentM?: number
  }
  diagnostics?: {
    degraded: boolean
    warnings: string[]
  }
}{noformat}

Error model expectations:

* VALIDATION_ERROR
* NO_RESULT
* AMBIGUOUS_LOCATION
* PROVIDER_FAILURE
* RATE_LIMITED
* NO_ROUTE_FOUND
* PARTIAL_SUCCESS

API design expectations:

* request/response objects must be stable enough for AI callers
* avoid UI-specific booleans like modal state or visual-only selection state
* service must be versionable without breaking callers

Suggested endpoint family (illustrative):

* POST /api/trip-planning/suggest
* POST /api/trip-planning/geocode
* POST /api/trip-planning/reverse-geocode
* POST /api/trip-planning/route
* POST /api/trip-planning/elevation
* POST /api/trip-planning/external-link

Observability requirements:

* structured logs for every provider call
* latency timing per provider operation
* correlation id propagation
* clear separation between caller-facing error and provider raw error

Acceptance criteria:

* a documented backend service contract exists for all core trip planning operations
* the service can be called without any dependency on React component state
* suggest/geocode/routing/elevation share a common validation and error model
* the design is reusable by UI, automation and AI callers
* implementation notes identify boundaries between provider adapter and Hobbeast domain service

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Define structured request and response schema for AI trip planning calls
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning AI and automation readiness
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Define a strict, versionable and machine-consumable schema for AI and automation initiated trip planning calls so that route generation is not based on ad-hoc prompts or UI-specific state.

Business context:
An AI-capable trip planning feature is only safe and maintainable if the contract is explicit. Agents and backend workflows must not rely on brittle text parsing, UI labels or implicit assumptions about current planner state.

Problem statement:
Without a stable schema, the same trip planning capability will behave inconsistently across UI, backend automation and future AI callers. A structured contract is required to make validation, auditing, retries and safe execution possible.

Primary goal:
Create a normalized request/response schema that can support manual UI usage, automation jobs and AI agent orchestration with the same underlying service contract.

Scope:

* define canonical input schema for trip planning
* define canonical output schema for routing results
* define optional fields for constraints and route preferences
* define error schema for machine handling
* define ambiguity and confirmation-needed schema
* define schema versioning rules
* define validation rules and required/optional field semantics

Detailed functional requirements:

# Request schema must support:

* route type
* start point
* end point
* waypoint list
* route constraints
* optional departure time
* optional user intent metadata
* optional planner mode metadata

# Response schema must support:

* resolved route summary
* geometry payload reference
* resolved point list in normalized form
* diagnostics and warnings
* route quality flags
* degradation indicators
* next-step recommendations when user confirmation is needed

# Error schema must support:

* validation failures
* ambiguous place resolution
* no route found
* provider failure
* rate limited
* partial success

# Schema must support the difference between:

* user asked for a route
* system needs clarification before route generation
* route created successfully but with warnings

Detailed technical requirements:

* define JSON-serializable request and response structures
* do not include UI-only concepts such as modal step index or component-local booleans
* schema must be stable enough for OpenAPI/JSON Schema style documentation later
* ambiguous location and disambiguation states must be explicit and typed
* schema should carry enough information to reconstruct and audit the decision path

Suggested request contract:

{noformat}export interface TripPlanningConstraintSet {
  avoidToll?: boolean
  avoidHighways?: boolean
  maxDistanceM?: number
  maxDurationS?: number
  requireLoop?: boolean
  preferScenic?: boolean
  preferEasyTerrain?: boolean
}

export interface AITripPlanningRequest {
  schemaVersion: '1.0'
  routeType: 'foot_hiking' | 'foot_fast' | 'bike_road' | 'bike_mountain' | 'car_fast' | 'car_short'
  start: LocationRef | UnresolvedLocationRef
  end: LocationRef | UnresolvedLocationRef
  waypoints?: Array<LocationRef | UnresolvedLocationRef>
  constraints?: TripPlanningConstraintSet
  departureAt?: string
  callerContext?: {
    source: 'ui' | 'automation' | 'ai_agent'
    eventId?: string
    userId?: string
  }
}{noformat}

Suggested response contract:

{noformat}export interface AITripPlanningResponse {
  schemaVersion: '1.0'
  status: 'success' | 'needs_clarification' | 'partial_success' | 'failure'
  resolvedRoute?: TripPlanningResult
  unresolvedItems?: Array<{
    field: 'start' | 'end' | 'waypoint'
    reason: 'ambiguous_location' | 'no_result' | 'invalid_constraint'
    candidates?: LocationRef[]
  }>
  warnings?: string[]
  diagnostics?: {
    provider: 'mapy'
    degraded: boolean
    correlationId?: string
  }
  error?: {
    code: 'VALIDATION_ERROR' | 'AMBIGUOUS_LOCATION' | 'NO_RESULT' | 'PROVIDER_FAILURE' | 'NO_ROUTE_FOUND' | 'RATE_LIMITED'
    message: string
  }
}{noformat}

Validation rules:

* start and end are mandatory for route planning execution
* routeType is mandatory
* waypoints may be empty but must preserve order if present
* unresolved items must not silently convert to arbitrary provider result
* response status must always be set
* partial_success and needs_clarification are first-class states, not generic errors

Acceptance criteria:

* a versioned AI/automation-friendly request schema exists
* a versioned response schema exists
* ambiguity, validation and degraded execution are explicitly represented
* the schema is usable without any dependency on the web UI structure
* the story description is sufficient for implementation of shared types and API documentation

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add AI-safe ambiguous location resolution workflow for trip planning
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning AI and automation readiness
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Prevent AI-driven or automated trip planning from silently selecting the wrong place when the input location is ambiguous, incomplete or underspecified.

Business context:
Location ambiguity is one of the highest-risk failure modes in trip planning. A human user may notice a wrong city, mountain peak or POI immediately, but an automation or AI agent can otherwise proceed with a plausible but incorrect route. Hobbeast therefore needs an explicit ambiguity-resolution workflow instead of optimistic best-guess behavior.

Problem statement:
Map-style suggest and geocoding systems often return multiple valid candidates for the same text input, for example:

* multiple cities with the same name
* POI vs street vs district ambiguity
* missing country or region context
* route points described only semantically, like "the lake viewpoint"
Without a dedicated resolution workflow, AI callers may create invalid or misleading routes.

Primary goal:
Introduce a deterministic and reviewable location-resolution process that can stop execution, return candidate options and request clarification when confidence is insufficient.

Scope:

* define unresolved location input model
* define ambiguity detection rules
* define candidate scoring and candidate list return structure
* define clarification-needed response path
* define confidence thresholds and execution blocking rules
* define UI-compatible and AI-compatible resolution payloads
* define fallback chain across suggest, geocode and reverse geocode driven workflows

Detailed functional requirements:

# The system must distinguish between:

* exact resolved location
* partially resolved location
* ambiguous location with multiple candidates
* no-result location
* invalid location input

# The system must not auto-execute a route if either start or end remains in ambiguous state above the configured confidence threshold.
# The system must return candidate options for ambiguous items with enough metadata for downstream selection:

* display label
* lat/lon
* location type
* region/country context
* provider id if available
* confidence score or ranking position

# The system must support explicit clarification on:

* start point
* end point
* any waypoint

# The system must support a fallback resolution chain such as:

* suggest candidates
* exact geocode refinement
* reverse geocode normalization if point already exists
* return needs_clarification if still ambiguous

# For AI callers, the system must be able to return a structured clarification object instead of plain text.
# The system must support safe execution only when:

* all mandatory locations are resolved
* ambiguity threshold is satisfied
* constraints remain valid after resolution

Detailed technical requirements:

* define {{UnresolvedLocationRef}} structure separate from {{LocationRef}}
* store candidate lists in deterministic order
* make confidence threshold configurable, not hardcoded in UI
* expose machine-readable reasons for ambiguity such as:
** MULTIPLE_CITY_MATCHES
** POI_AND_ADDRESS_CONFLICT
** COUNTRY_CONTEXT_MISSING
** INSUFFICIENT_PROVIDER_CONFIDENCE
* resolution logic must be reusable by both manual UI flow and AI flow
* do not collapse candidate lists into a single best guess without traceability

Suggested type model:

{noformat}export interface UnresolvedLocationRef {
  inputText: string
  source?: 'typed_text' | 'ai_generated' | 'map_click' | 'imported'
}

export interface LocationCandidate {
  label: string
  lat: number
  lon: number
  type: 'address' | 'poi' | 'city' | 'district' | 'region' | 'unknown'
  region?: string
  country?: string
  providerId?: string
  confidence?: number
}

export interface LocationResolutionResult {
  status: 'resolved' | 'needs_clarification' | 'no_result' | 'invalid'
  resolved?: LocationRef
  candidates?: LocationCandidate[]
  reason?: 'MULTIPLE_CITY_MATCHES' | 'POI_AND_ADDRESS_CONFLICT' | 'COUNTRY_CONTEXT_MISSING' | 'NO_PROVIDER_RESULT' | 'INVALID_INPUT'
}{noformat}

Decision rules:

* if exactly one candidate is above confident threshold and no close peer candidate exists, status may be {{resolved}}
* if multiple candidates are close in score, status must be {{needs_clarification}}
* if no candidate exists, status must be {{no_result}}
* if input is blank or structurally unusable, status must be {{invalid}}

Example scenario coverage:

* "Springfield" without country => needs clarification
* "Margit-sziget" with one dominant Budapest result => may auto-resolve if threshold satisfied
* "lake viewpoint" in remote hiking context => likely ambiguous unless contextualized by prior route area

Acceptance criteria:

* ambiguous locations are represented as a first-class state, not hidden fallback behavior
* AI and automation callers receive structured clarification payloads when required
* the routing service does not continue with silently guessed mandatory points
* candidate ordering and ambiguity reasons are explicit enough for implementation and QA
* story description is sufficient for implementing shared types, service logic and clarification UX/API behavior

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add AI-assisted trip generation and waypoint suggestion readiness
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning AI and automation readiness
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Prepare the trip planning domain so that future AI-assisted route generation can propose start/end combinations, waypoint candidates and route alternatives without requiring a redesign of the core planner architecture.

Business context:
The current business value is not only in manual route creation but in future higher-level assistance such as:

* plan a scenic hike between two places
* suggest two intermediate viewpoints
* make the route easier or shorter
* create a family-friendly version of the route
To support this safely, the trip planning architecture must preserve separation between deterministic routing and optional recommendation logic.

Problem statement:
If AI-assisted route generation is bolted on top of the existing planner without preparation, the result will be brittle, hard to validate and difficult to audit. The planner needs explicit extension points for AI-generated waypoint candidates, route alternatives and optimization constraints.

Primary goal:
Define the readiness layer that allows AI-generated proposals to be inserted, reviewed, ranked and either accepted or rejected without bypassing deterministic routing and validation.

Scope:

* define proposal model for AI-suggested points and route variants
* define distinction between suggested point and accepted route point
* define planner readiness for route alternatives
* define metadata model for suggestion rationale
* define validation pass that every AI proposal must go through before becoming an executable route request
* define hooks for future scoring of scenic, easier, shorter or thematic routes

Out of scope:

* full production AI generation model
* semantic content generation for travel articles or full travel itineraries
* autonomous final decision making without validation

Detailed functional requirements:

# The system must support proposed points that are not yet accepted into the saved route.
# The system must support multiple route alternatives for the same high-level user intent.
# Every AI-suggested point must pass the same location resolution workflow as manual input.
# Every AI-suggested route must pass the same deterministic routing validation as manual routing.
# The planner must preserve the difference between:

* suggested waypoint
* accepted waypoint
* rejected waypoint
* alternative route candidate

# The system must support rationale metadata such as:

* scenic viewpoint
* shorter detour
* easier terrain
* avoids steep climb
* avoids road traffic

# The system must support user review or workflow review before committing suggestions into saved trip data.

Detailed technical requirements:

* define proposal entities or in-memory structures separate from final trip plan entities
* preserve traceability of where each suggestion came from
* do not allow AI-generated waypoints to bypass validation, ambiguity resolution or route recalculation
* support suggestion ranking and future confidence scores
* support later UI rendering of multiple route alternatives

Suggested data model concepts:

{noformat}export interface TripPointProposal {
  id: string
  source: 'ai_agent' | 'automation_rule' | 'manual_helper'
  proposalType: 'start' | 'end' | 'waypoint'
  candidate: LocationRef | UnresolvedLocationRef
  rationale?: string[]
  confidence?: number
  status: 'proposed' | 'accepted' | 'rejected'
}

export interface RouteAlternativeProposal {
  id: string
  basedOnRequestId: string
  routeType: string
  proposedWaypoints: TripPointProposal[]
  expectedLengthM?: number
  expectedDurationS?: number
  rationale?: string[]
  status: 'proposed' | 'accepted' | 'rejected'
}{noformat}

Decision flow expectations:

# AI or automation proposes route enhancement or full route candidate
# proposed points are location-resolved and validated
# deterministic routing is executed
# result is returned as one or more route alternatives
# user or workflow accepts one route alternative
# only accepted alternative becomes persisted trip plan

Example scenarios:

* user asks for a more scenic hiking route => system proposes two alternative waypoint sets
* user asks for a shorter route => system proposes a reduced waypoint version
* AI suggests a lake viewpoint, but the location is ambiguous => clarification required before routing continues

Acceptance criteria:

* the architecture clearly separates proposed and accepted trip planning data
* AI or automation suggestions cannot silently become persisted route points without validation
* the story provides enough detail for implementing proposal models and future alternative-route workflows
* the design supports later UI and agent features without requiring core planner redesign

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add agent governance, quota control and audit trail for automated trip planning
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning AI and automation readiness
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Ensure that automated and AI-driven trip planning executions are governable, rate-aware and auditable, so Hobbeast can safely support non-human callers without losing operational control.

Business context:
As soon as trip planning becomes callable by automation or AI agents, the system needs stronger controls than a pure user-interaction feature. Without audit, caller identity, quota rules and execution traceability, the platform risks abuse, debugging difficulties, excessive provider usage and unreviewable autonomous actions.

Problem statement:
Manual user clicks are naturally limited and attributable. Automated trip planning is not. If the system later allows background jobs or AI agents to trigger geocoding, routing and elevation flows repeatedly, it must know:

* who triggered the action
* why it was triggered
* how often it can run
* what provider usage it consumed
* what final route result was chosen

Primary goal:
Define the governance and control layer that makes AI/automation trip planning operationally safe before large-scale automated use is introduced.

Scope:

* define caller identity model for UI, automation and AI agent executions
* define per-caller and per-scope quota concepts
* define audit log model for trip planning requests and decisions
* define execution trace model for provider calls and route outcomes
* define safe defaults for throttling and quota exhaustion behavior
* define reviewability for accepted vs rejected AI-proposed routes
* define security boundaries for secret usage and privileged execution paths

Detailed functional requirements:

# Every automated or AI-initiated trip planning execution must record:

* request id
* caller type
* caller id where available
* event id if relevant
* timestamp
* route intent summary
* final status

# The system must distinguish between caller categories:

* UI user action
* internal automation job
* AI agent
* admin/internal tooling

# The system must support configurable quotas or limits such as:

* per minute/provider call limit
* per day/agent call budget
* per event planning retry limit
* per workflow execution cap

# The system must define behavior when quota is reached:

* hard reject
* defer
* degrade to no-elevation mode
* require manual retry

# The system must record decision trail data for AI-assisted planning, including:

* original structured request
* ambiguity state if any
* proposed alternatives
* selected alternative
* accepted/rejected proposals

# Audit data must make it possible to answer:

* who/what created this route?
* was the route fully manual, automated or AI-assisted?
* what provider calls were executed?
* why was one route alternative accepted?

Detailed technical requirements:

* define audit entity separate from the final persisted trip plan
* audit structure must be append-oriented and safe for later analytics
* do not store secrets or unsafe provider payloads in audit log
* support correlation id across trip planning request, provider calls and saved result
* design must be compatible with future observability dashboards

Suggested audit model:

{noformat}export interface TripPlanningAuditRecord {
  id: string
  requestId: string
  callerType: 'ui' | 'automation' | 'ai_agent' | 'admin_tool'
  callerId?: string
  eventId?: string
  startedAt: string
  finishedAt?: string
  status: 'success' | 'needs_clarification' | 'partial_success' | 'failure' | 'rate_limited'
  routeType?: string
  provider: 'mapy'
  requestSummary: {
    hasWaypoints: boolean
    waypointCount: number
    hasConstraints: boolean
  }
  chosenAlternativeId?: string
  warnings?: string[]
  errorCode?: string
  correlationId?: string
}{noformat}

Suggested quota model concepts:

{noformat}export interface TripPlanningQuotaPolicy {
  callerType: 'automation' | 'ai_agent' | 'admin_tool'
  maxCallsPerMinute?: number
  maxCallsPerDay?: number
  maxElevationCallsPerDay?: number
  maxRetriesPerRequest?: number
}{noformat}

Operational design expectations:

* governance checks occur before provider-intensive execution
* quota breaches return typed responses, not generic failures
* audit trail is written for both success and failure paths
* accepted route choice is traceable when alternatives were proposed

Acceptance criteria:

* automated and AI-initiated trip planning has an explicit audit model
* quota and throttling concepts are defined with caller-aware rules
* trip planning decisions are traceable from request to chosen result
* the story description is detailed enough for implementing governance entities, checks and audit persistence

importedJiraIssues.summary: Story: Add AI-assisted trip generation and waypoint suggestion readiness
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Mapy.com trip planning AI and automation readiness
importedJiraIssues.description:
Objective:
Prepare the trip planning domain so that future AI-assisted route generation can propose start and end combinations, waypoint candidates and route alternatives without requiring a redesign of the core planner architecture.

Business context:
The long-term value of trip planning is not limited to manual route creation. Future assistant features may need to support requests such as:

* suggest a scenic hike between two places
* add one or two viewpoint stops
* generate a shorter or easier variant of the same route
* propose a family-friendly route option
To support this safely, Hobbeast must separate deterministic routing from suggestion and recommendation logic.

Problem statement:
If AI-generated suggestions are inserted directly into the planner as final route data, the system becomes hard to validate, hard to audit and unsafe to automate. The planner needs explicit proposal states and validation gates.

Primary goal:
Create the readiness layer that allows AI-generated points and route alternatives to be proposed, validated, reviewed and then accepted or rejected.

Scope:

* define proposal model for AI-suggested points
* define proposal model for alternative routes
* define distinction between proposed and accepted route points
* define validation pass required before any proposed point becomes part of an executable route request
* define metadata model for rationale and confidence
* define readiness hooks for route alternatives such as scenic, easier, shorter and thematic options

Out of scope:

* full production AI generation engine
* free-form natural language itinerary writing
* autonomous final route acceptance without validation or review

Detailed functional requirements:

# The system must support proposed points that are not yet committed into the final trip plan.
# The system must support more than one route alternative for the same user intent.
# Every AI-suggested point must go through the same location resolution workflow as manual input.
# Every AI-suggested route must go through the same deterministic routing validation as manual routing.
# The planner must preserve the difference between:

* proposed waypoint
* accepted waypoint
* rejected waypoint
* proposed route alternative
* accepted route alternative

# The system must support rationale metadata such as:

* scenic viewpoint
* shorter detour
* easier terrain
* avoids steep climb
* avoids traffic-heavy section

# The system must support a review step where a user or workflow accepts one proposal before persistence.

Detailed technical requirements:

* proposal entities must be stored or represented separately from final trip plan entities
* proposal origin must be traceable (ai_agent, automation_rule, manual_assistant, etc.)
* no AI-generated point may bypass ambiguity resolution, validation or route recalculation
* proposal structures must support confidence scores and ranking later
* route alternatives must be renderable later in UI without changing the underlying trip planning contract

Suggested model:

{noformat}export interface TripPointProposal {
  id: string
  source: 'ai_agent' | 'automation_rule' | 'manual_helper'
  proposalType: 'start' | 'end' | 'waypoint'
  candidate: LocationRef | UnresolvedLocationRef
  rationale?: string[]
  confidence?: number
  status: 'proposed' | 'accepted' | 'rejected'
}

export interface RouteAlternativeProposal {
  id: string
  basedOnRequestId: string
  routeType: string
  proposedWaypoints: TripPointProposal[]
  expectedLengthM?: number
  expectedDurationS?: number
  rationale?: string[]
  status: 'proposed' | 'accepted' | 'rejected'
}{noformat}

Decision flow expectations:

# AI or automation proposes one or more route improvements or full alternatives
# proposed points are location-resolved and validated
# deterministic routing is executed for each viable candidate
# route alternatives are returned as proposals
# a user or workflow accepts one alternative
# only the accepted alternative becomes the persisted trip plan

Acceptance criteria:

* the architecture explicitly separates proposed and accepted trip planning data
* AI or automation suggestions cannot silently become final route points without validation
* the story provides enough detail to implement proposal models and route alternative readiness
* the design supports later UI and agent features without requiring core planner redesign

importedJiraIssues.summary: Subtask: Implement marker and route overlay architecture in TripPlanningMap
importedJiraIssues.parentSummary: Story: Select and implement web map renderer for Mapy.com trip planning

* marker rendering contract for start, end and waypoint roles

* idle
* loading
* results available
* no results
* request failed
